market_session: US equity market holiday (no regular session)
text: US equity markets were closed on Friday, June 19, 2026 for Juneteenth National Independence Day. The NYSE holiday calendar lists Juneteenth on Friday, June 19, and the Nasdaq US holiday schedule lists June 19, 2026 as Closed, so there was no regular US cash equity session on the report date.

The US bond market was also closed on June 19, 2026. SIFMA's 2026 holiday schedule lists Juneteenth (Friday, June 19) as a full close for trading in US dollar-denominated fixed-income securities, with no early-close time attached.

market_session: next US regular session after the Juneteenth holiday
text: US equity markets reopen for regular trading on Monday, June 22, 2026, the next session after the Juneteenth closure.

market_session: US regular session close on 2026-06-18 (last session before the Juneteenth holiday)

calculation.method: reported close and daily move from AP closing-index ledger

text: Going into the holiday, the last US regular session (Thursday, June 18, 2026) closed higher: the S&P 500 rose 80.48 points (1.1%) to 7,500.58; the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 72.15 points (0.1%) to 51,564.70; the Nasdaq Composite rose 496.28 points (1.9%) to 26,517.93; and the Russell 2000 rose 61.78 points (2.1%) to 2,979.77.

market_session: next scheduled US market holiday after Juneteenth
text: The next scheduled US equity-market closure after Juneteenth is Friday, July 3, 2026 (Independence Day observed); SIFMA recommends a 2:00 p.m. ET early bond-market close the prior day, Thursday, July 2, 2026.

value.level_basis: Trading Economics rounded index level; a precise historical close was not yet posted by the primary vendor (Yahoo Finance) at access time

market_session: European regular close; Trading Economics EU50-page Friday wrap level (rounded) with Reuters and RTTNews percent moves; currency EUR

calculation.method: Trading Economics Friday level 636 vs prior Yahoo close 637.14; Reuters and Trading Economics report -0.2% while RTTNews reports -0.24%, bracketing the daily move

text: The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 inched down from a record-high week, easing to about 636 on Trading Economics's Friday level, with the daily decline reported at 0.2% by Reuters and Trading Economics and 0.24% by RTTNews, from a 637.14 prior close; it still held a roughly 0.4% weekly gain.

value.level_basis: Trading Economics rounded EU50 index level; precise vendor historical close not yet posted at access time

market_session: European regular close; Trading Economics EU50 Friday wrap (rounded); currency EUR

calculation.method: Trading Economics Friday EU50 level 6,302 at -0.3% vs prior Yahoo close 6,323.27

text: The blue-chip EURO STOXX 50 eased about 0.3% to roughly 6,302 on Trading Economics's Friday level, down from a 6,323.27 prior close, paring part of a strong week.

calculation.method: Trading Economics DE40 Friday level 24,986 at -0.16% vs prior close 25,026.80; RTTNews independently reports the DAX down 0.16%

text: Germany's DAX drifted down about 0.16% to roughly 24,986 on Trading Economics's Friday level, snapping a six-day advance in a session marked by quarterly futures-and-options expiry; RTTNews independently reported the DAX down 0.16%, from a 25,026.80 prior close.

value.level_basis: Trading Economics FR40 level; RTTNews independently corroborates the -0.55% move

market_session: Euronext Paris regular close; Trading Economics FR40 level corroborated by RTTNews percent; currency EUR

calculation.method: Trading Economics FR40 Friday level 8,421 at -0.55% vs prior close 8,467.98; RTTNews independently reports the CAC 40 down 0.55%

text: France's CAC 40 was the weakest major bourse, losing 0.55% to about 8,421 on Trading Economics's Friday level after US-Iran talks in Switzerland were called off; RTTNews independently reported the CAC 40 down 0.55%, from an 8,467.98 prior close.

market_session: London regular close; Investors Chronicle/LSEG historical daily row (data as of 16:45 BST, 15-min delayed); RTTNews corroborates -0.35%; currency GBP

calculation.method: June 19 Investors Chronicle close 10,363.27 minus June 18 close 10,399.70 equals -36.43 points, -0.35%, matching the tearsheet header and RTTNews

text: The FTSE 100 fell 36.43 points, or 0.35%, from 10,399.70 to 10,363.27 on the Investors Chronicle/LSEG historical daily basis on June 19, 2026; RTTNews independently reported the FTSE 100 down 0.35%.

value.primary_pressure: US-Iran talks scheduled in Switzerland abruptly cancelled after fresh Israel-Hezbollah hostilities; a Lebanon ceasefire was later reported
value.us_session: no US trading - Juneteenth federal holiday; US markets closed
value.macro: German producer prices rose 2.2% year-on-year in May, less than expected

market_session: European regular session, Friday June 19, 2026; close wraps (Reuters, RTTNews) with US Juneteenth holiday context (Dow Jones/Morningstar)
text: European shares inched lower on Friday, pulling back from record highs set earlier in the week, as risk appetite cooled after US-Iran peace talks scheduled in Switzerland were abruptly cancelled amid fresh Israel-Hezbollah hostilities (a Lebanon ceasefire was reported later in the day), with US markets closed for the Juneteenth holiday. Mining stocks led sector losses, down about 2.1% as metals prices eased, and travel and leisure fell about 0.9% on firmer oil, while energy rose about 1.3%; Switzerland's SMI edged up 0.06%. German producer prices rose 2.2% year on year in May, less than expected.

value.uk_miners: Antofagasta and Pan African Resources among the worst hit
value.corporate: PPHE Hotel Group slid after $1.2bn takeover talks ended

market_session: European regular session close, Friday June 19, 2026; single-stock moves from Trading Economics country wraps and the Reuters close wrap
text: Dispersion was led by luxury and mining. Hermes fell about 2.3% and L'Oreal about 1.9%; LVMH was down about 1.2% on the France wrap, while the pan-European wrap grouped LVMH, Hermes and Ferrari in a 2.3%-2.5% decline. Tech names ASML and Prosus slipped about 1% and 2.2%. In Germany, Volkswagen dropped more than 4% as it traded ex-dividend, while defense names Rheinmetall (+1.8%), Renk (+1.5%) and Hensoldt (+1.5%) gained and Mercedes-Benz rose about 1.9% on reports of planned EU tariffs on Chinese plug-in hybrids. In France, Airbus (-1.3%) and Safran (-1.2%) weakened while TotalEnergies added about 1%. London-listed miners Antofagasta and Pan African Resources were among the worst hit, and PPHE Hotel Group slid after $1.2 billion takeover talks ended.

market_session: Week ending Friday June 19, 2026; weekly index performance from Trading Economics area/country wraps and Reuters
text: Despite Friday's pullback, the major indices ended the week higher: the STOXX Europe 600 rose about 0.4%, the EURO STOXX 50 gained about 1.9%, and the DAX advanced about 1.6%, helped by earlier-week optimism over US-Iran de-escalation and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

value.recent_action: the ECB raised rates the prior week, its first hike in nearly three years
value.july_expectation: policymakers broadly expected a pause at the July meeting
value.hawkish_dissent: ECB Governing Council member Pierre Wunsch said the ECB could raise again as soon as next month if inflation broadened beyond energy
value.fed_context: the new Warsh-led FOMC left US rates unchanged on June 17 and signaled a possible hike later in 2026

market_session: Policy backdrop as of the June 19, 2026 European session; the ECB action is dated to the prior week
text: On policy, the European Central Bank had raised interest rates the previous week, its first hike in nearly three years, and most policymakers were seen pausing in July, though Governing Council member Pierre Wunsch said the ECB could move again as soon as next month if inflation spread beyond energy. Across the Atlantic, the new Warsh-led Federal Reserve left US rates unchanged on June 17 and signaled a possible further hike later in 2026, a hawkish backdrop that continued to temper European risk appetite.

market_session: Japan equity market close; Nikkei Indexes official daily-summary basis

calculation.method: reported close, daily point change, percent change, open, high, low, and previous close from Nikkei Indexes official daily summary

text: Nikkei Indexes showed the Nikkei 225 closed June 19, 2026 at 71,250.06, up 196.57 points or 0.28%, a new record close, with an intraday range of 70,517.98 to 71,952.99 and a previous close of 71,053.49.

market_session: Japan equity market close; Nikkei Indexes official daily-summary basis
text: Nikkei Indexes reported 66 advancers versus 157 decliners and 2 unchanged among Nikkei 225 constituents, with Technology contributing +283.89 yen and Materials +148.75 yen while Consumer Goods subtracted 167.16 yen and Financials subtracted 42.75 yen, indicating a narrow technology-led advance despite the record close.

market_session: Japan equity market close; Investing.com TOPIX historical-data and CNBC-cited basis

calculation.method: reported close, open, and percent change from Investing.com TOPIX historical table cross-checked against CNBC market report

text: Investing.com and CNBC showed the broader TOPIX index fell 0.57% to close at 4,044.96 on June 19, 2026, diverging from the Nikkei 225's record close.

market_session: Korea regular-session close; Investing.com KOSPI historical-data basis

calculation.method: reported close, daily point change, percent change, open, high, low, previous close, and volume from Investing.com KOSPI historical table

text: Investing.com's KOSPI historical table showed a June 19, 2026 close of 9,052.42, down 11.42 points or 0.13% from the prior record close of 9,063.84, with an intraday range of 8,831.72 to 9,385.59 and volume of 511.08M.

market_session: Korea regular-session close; CNBC and Investing.com basis
text: CNBC reported Samsung Electronics reversed earlier gains and fell 2.34% while SK Hynix rose 2.94% and the small-cap Kosdaq dropped 3.43%; the KOSPI itself opened at 9,288.89, rose to an intraday high of 9,385.59, then plunged to a low of 8,831.72 before recovering to close at 9,052.42.

market_session: No session; Greater China markets closed for Dragon Boat Festival
text: Markets in Hong Kong, Shanghai and Taiwan were closed on June 19, 2026 for the Dragon Boat Festival; Trading Economics and the AP market wrap confirmed the closure, with Shanghai's last close at 4,090.48 and the Hang Seng-linked quote last at 23,924.81 on June 18.

subject.index_group: tracked Asia indexes with June 19 session

market_session: Asia local market closes for June 19, 2026

calculation.method: compared reported daily percent changes for the three tracked Asia indexes that held a regular session on June 19, 2026

text: On June 19, 2026, three of the six tracked Asia indexes were closed for the Dragon Boat Festival; among the three that traded, the Nikkei 225 was strongest at +0.28% (record close) while the TOPIX was weakest at -0.57%, and the KOSPI closed down 0.13% after a volatile intraday reversal.

SIFMA recommended a full close of the U.S. dollar-denominated fixed income market on Friday, June 19, 2026 for Juneteenth, and U.S. equity cash markets were closed, so there was no live U.S. Treasury or Wall Street session and no June 19 Treasury par-yield print; the day's macro signal therefore came from global FX, ICE Brent and European cash rates.

calculation.method: read the official Treasury par-yield CSV; 10Y minus 2Y for the last available print; confirmed no 06/19/2026 row exists

text: With the U.S. fixed income market closed for Juneteenth, the official Treasury par-yield CSV showed no June 19, 2026 row; the last print remained June 18 at 2-year 4.19%, 10-year 4.46% and 30-year 4.90%, a +27 bp 2s10s spread (versus +29 bp on June 17), so U.S. rates were effectively frozen at Thursday's close through the holiday.

value.weekly_context: strongest weekly gain in about a month, mostly at the yen's expense

calculation.method: vendor close vs prior session; intraday high cross-checked against the CNBC 52-week-high stamp dated 06/19/26 and the Reuters 13-month-high description

text: The U.S. Dollar Index ended Friday, June 19, 2026 little changed near 100.80 (about -0.05%) after printing a fresh 13-month/52-week intraday high of 101.13 (low 100.70) versus the June 18 close of 100.849; FX traded through the U.S. cash holiday and the dollar logged its strongest weekly gain in roughly a month, mostly at the yen's expense.

value.vendor_note: Trading Economics 2.96% (+4 bp) vs WSJ 2.992% (+6 bp); roughly 3 bp vendor spread disclosed

calculation.method: vendor 10Y Bund yield vs prior session; Trading Economics cross-checked against WSJ; Bundesbank daily-yield table cited as official primary

text: Germany's 10-year Bund yield rose on June 19, 2026 to roughly 2.96%-2.99% - Trading Economics dated it 2.96% (+4 bp) and the Wall Street Journal 2.992% (+6 bp), about a 3 bp vendor spread - extending a modest move higher into the European close while U.S. rates were frozen by the holiday; the Deutsche Bundesbank daily-yield table for 19.06.2026 is the official primary reference.

value.vendor_note: FT 4.84 (+0.003, ~flat) vs WSJ 4.846% (+0.078); level agrees, daily-change basis differs

calculation.method: vendor 10Y gilt yield; FT end-of-day 18:30 BST tearsheet cross-checked against WSJ

text: The UK 10-year gilt yield was about 4.84% at the London close on June 19, 2026 (FT 4.84, +0.003 pp at 18:30 BST; WSJ 4.846%, +0.078 pp) - broadly steady at an elevated level with the gilt market open through the U.S. Juneteenth holiday, the vendor disagreement confined to the size of the daily change rather than the level.

subject.name: Germany producer price index, industrial products (domestic sales)

value.release: Destatis press release No. 212 of 19 June 2026

calculation.method: official Destatis y/y and m/m producer-price change for May 2026; breakdown reconciled with Trading Economics

text: Germany's producer prices for industrial products rose 2.2% year on year and 0.3% on the month in May 2026, Destatis reported on June 19, 2026 (press release No. 212) - the fastest annual pace since May 2023 but below the 2.5% y/y and 0.7% m/m forecasts. The rise was led by intermediate goods (+4.2%) and energy (+2.5% y/y), with mineral-oil products up 34.9% y/y on the Iran/Middle East conflict (naphtha +60.9%, heating oil +52.8%, motor fuels +24.2%); excluding energy, producer prices rose 2.3% y/y, while non-durable consumer goods fell 1.7%.

value.weekly_context: sharp week-long selloff tied to the U.S.-Iran deal reopening the Strait of Hormuz; oil dipped below $80 intraday then erased losses after the Iran talks were called off
value.us_market_note: NYMEX WTI traded only on CME's shortened Juneteenth session

calculation.method: vendor front-month Brent close vs prior session; Yahoo BZ=F cross-checked against Trading Economics

text: Brent crude rebounded on Friday, June 19, 2026, ending near $80.59/bbl, up about $0.74 or 0.93% (range $78.80-$80.82; Trading Economics cross-checks $80.58) after dipping below $80 intraday and erasing earlier losses when U.S. Vice President JD Vance pulled out of Friday's Iran talks. The bounce capped a sharp week-long selloff driven by the U.S.-Iran deal reopening the Strait of Hormuz, while U.S. WTI traded only on CME's shortened Juneteenth session.

value.us_iran: U.S. VP JD Vance pulled out of planned Friday talks with Iranian negotiators in Switzerland
value.lebanon: Israel and Hezbollah agreed a ceasefire in Lebanon, per a U.S. official
value.hormuz: tankers began transiting the Strait of Hormuz after the U.S. lifted its blockade on Iran on Thursday
value.yen: near a 40-year low, weakest since late 1986, down 5 of the last 6 weeks; Tokyo intervention warnings

text: The June 19, 2026 macro session was set by geopolitics and FX rather than U.S. data, with U.S. cash markets shut for Juneteenth. Reuters reported global shares dipped (MSCI All-World -0.15%, STOXX Europe -0.12%, U.S. futures -0.1% to -0.2%) after VP JD Vance pulled out of Friday peace talks with Iran in Switzerland, even as Israel and Hezbollah agreed a Lebanon ceasefire and tankers resumed transit through the Strait of Hormuz following Thursday's lifting of the U.S. blockade; the yen sat near a 40-year low (weakest since late 1986) on a five-of-six-week slide, drawing intervention warnings from Tokyo.

US equity, bond and over-the-counter markets were closed on Friday June 19 for the Juneteenth federal holiday, with the NYSE and Nasdaq shut and Federal Reserve banking services not operating; normal trading resumes Monday June 22. The authoritative US risk-sentiment prints for the date are therefore the June 18 close carried through the weekend rather than fresh June 19 cash readings, and the live read on June 19 came from the open European and Asian sessions.

The last US risk-sentiment print carried into the holiday was a strong tech-led relief rally on June 18: the S&P 500 rose 1.08% to 7,500.58, the Nasdaq Composite gained 1.91% to 26,517.93, and the Dow added 0.14% (about 72 points) to 51,564.70. The rally came after the June 17 Fed-driven sell-off (the Fed meeting pointed to a possible late-2026 rate hike) and on US-Iran de-escalation as Washington ended its Iran blockade, framing June 19 as a pause-and-reassess session rather than a fresh leg higher.

European risk sentiment softened on June 19: the pan-European STOXX 600 closed about 0.2% lower, easing back from record highs hit earlier in the week but still holding a roughly 0.4% weekly gain, as investors reacted to the postponement of the US-Iran technical talks and fresh Israel-Hezbollah hostilities in southern Lebanon before the ceasefire took hold.

London's FTSE 100 fell 0.34% (about 35 points) to close at 10,364, with losses led by miners Antofagasta (-6.16%) and Fresnillo (-5.36%) plus Unite Group (-4.23%), partly offset by energy and defensives BP (+2.08%), Relx (+1.43%) and Informa (+1.40%) as risk appetite faded into the weekend.

Sector breadth in Europe was defensive-tilted and commodity-driven: basic resources/mining fell 2.1% as metals prices eased (Antofagasta -6.1%, Rio Tinto -2.57%, Anglo American -2.63%, Glencore -1.6%, and BHP -4.3% after flagging a roughly $2.3 billion impairment on its Jansen potash project), travel and leisure dropped 0.9% on the firmer oil, while energy advanced 1.3% (BP +2.81%, Shell higher) - a rotation consistent with a market re-pricing geopolitical and commodity risk rather than a broad risk-off.

UK 10-year gilt yields rose about 8 basis points to 4.83% (with the 2-year up more than 6 bps to a one-week high of 4.25%) after public-sector borrowing reached £23.3 billion in May - above expectations and the highest May reading since 2019 - while retail sales rebounded 1.2% and Labour's Andy Burnham won the Greater Manchester mayoral by-election, adding a fiscal-risk overlay to the European sentiment backdrop.

Asian sentiment was led by a record close in Tokyo: Japan's Nikkei 225 rose 0.28% to a fresh all-time high of 71,250.06, even as the broader Topix slipped 0.57% to about 4,044.96 in mixed trade, after data showed core inflation steady at 1.4%. For the week the Nikkei and Topix advanced roughly 8% and 4% respectively as the US-Iran peace agreement drove oil lower and eased inflation worries.

Vendor-drift disclosure: the authoritative Nikkei 225 cash close used here is 71,250.06 (+0.28%) on a CNBC/AP basis, while Trading Economics' real-time JP225 feed showed a higher quote (roughly 71,773 to an intraday record near 71,952.99, a +1.0% to +1.3% daily-change basis). The gap reflects index-provider real-time/intraday pricing versus official cash settlement, not a contradiction in direction; both agree the index set a fresh record. The cash close is treated as primary per the pipeline's vendor-drift discipline.

Brent crude was up 0.25% (about $0.20) at $80.05 a barrel by 10:55 AM ET on June 19, and WTI rose 0.33% to $76.85, in light holiday-thinned volume - but Brent remained on track for a weekly fall of more than 8% as the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire and rising Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic outweighed lingering risk, with US sanctions on Iranian oil being lifted. Phil Flynn of Price Futures Group noted 'the backlog of ships can move quicker than some people think.'

subject.name: Ceasefire and Hormuz reopening
subject.event: Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire and Strait of Hormuz tanker restart

text: The Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire took effect at 4 PM local time (1300 GMT) on Friday after an earlier exchange of fire, and at least four tankers carrying crude, products and LPG entered the Strait of Hormuz heading for Iraqi Gulf ports. The southern (Omani-waters) and northern (Iranian-waters) routes appeared fully open, though the main central route still had an estimated 80 mines to clear - a partial, conditions-bound reopening that supported the week's oil-price relief while leaving execution risk on the table.

subject.name: June 19 dominant sentiment driver
subject.event: US-Iran interim deal signed; Switzerland talks postponed amid Lebanon flare-up

text: The dominant sentiment driver on June 19 was a relief-rally narrative intact but visibly tempered by execution risk: the US and Iran had signed an interim memorandum of understanding digitally to halt fighting and reopen the Strait of Hormuz (with Iran's leadership approving it despite reservations and US sanctions on Iranian oil being lifted), yet the technical follow-up talks scheduled for Switzerland on Friday were postponed and Vice President JD Vance delayed his trip after fighting flared in southern Lebanon, where Israeli strikes killed at least 18 people before the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire took hold. Markets read this as the deal holding but its seamless execution not yet proven.

A hawkish counter-current ran through global rate sentiment: the Bank of Japan had raised its benchmark rate by 25 bps to a 31-year high of 1% on June 16 in a 7-1 vote and signaled further hikes. Data released Friday June 19 showed Japan's core CPI (ex-fresh food) up 1.4% year-on-year in May, in line with forecasts, while 'core-core' inflation eased to 1.8% from 1.9% and Tokyo core CPI sat at 1.3% - a fourth month below the BOJ's 2% target that complicates the pace of further tightening even as the BOJ diverges from a Fed now signaling hike risk.

The cross-asset risk map on June 19 was mixed-to-cautiously-risk-off into the weekend: European equities dipped (STOXX 600 -0.2%, FTSE 100 -0.34%) on mining weakness, Asia was split with Japan at a record high against broader regional caution, US cash markets were shut for Juneteenth (last print the June 18 tech-led rally), Brent eked out a small intraday gain but stayed down about 8% on the week, and UK gilt yields backed up on fiscal data. The picture was of a market pausing to re-price the durability and execution of the US-Iran deal rather than reversing the week's relief rally, with the BOJ's tightening adding a hawkish rates undercurrent.
